sentence: Okay, first of all, I have not bought this system. I actually was just looking at the reviews left by others because the system is built using a processor that I am considering using in a PC I build myself. The Processor scores well in benchmarks and is highly rated by the people who have bought it and when I saw that this pre-built system was so poorly rated it made me wonder why that is. What I then discovered is that with the exception of one every review is left by someone who, like me, prefers to build his/her own system and who also is unable to wrap his mind around the idea that not everyone is interested in building their own computer nor the fact that many people have enough money to be able to afford to pay others (like those same reviewers, should they wish to go into a computer building business, for instance) who know how to build PCs and enjoy doing so. Reading their comments I would almost think they were the work of only one person using different accounts because the sole complaint seems to be that this computer is sold with a markup of the price it would cost to buy the components and assemble it oneself. Odd that 4 or 5 reviewers should all be so single-minded and all so disregarding of the disservice they are doing to other consumers who are trying to compare computers by lowering the rating this product receives for reasons that have nothing to do with the quality or performance of the product. It doesn't seem to occur to them that the people who are looking at the ratings of pre-built systems will for the most part be people who have no intention of building their own computer and are not interested in doing so regardless of the opinions held by a group of rogue reviewers. Therefore I have given the system a five star rating in an attempt to return this product to an overall rating that is more reflective of it's value. I am not making an in depth analysis of the product but it has a processor that has received a great deal of praise and performs well when objectively tested. The people in the market for a computer and the seller of this system both deserve better than to have the ratings system rendered useless by people who either don't understand the point of that system or who are just bent on ... I don't know ... some vigilante cause devoted to promoting the idea that everyone must build his or her own computer? Hard to say -- it's strange.
label: Positive Review

sentence:
Before I get into too much detail about this laptop let me tell you, this is a MUST buy for ANYONE who wants a FAST, POWRFUL, or INEXPENSIVE computer to use. This is an OUTSTANDING laptop, with only a few cons that I will go over. But first, the good stuff:

PROS:

* It's VERY fast! With the quad core i7 processer and 8GB of ram, you never have to worry about this laptop slowing down. You can run multiple applications at the same time, and it still won't break a sweat.

* There's PLENTY of space! This laptop comes with an 1TB hard drive, though you only get to use about 900GB, after Windows 8 software is installed, it's still more than enough space for music, movies and games.

* GREAT graphics card! Most benchmark websites will rate the \\"NVidia GeForce GT 650M\\" as a mid-ranged graphics card. However I have, Skyrim, Borderlands 2, Far Cry 3, Civilization 5 and many more games, running on High settings and I get 50-60 FPS with each game. For some people reading this and not understanding what I'm saying, it means I can run those games with High graphic settings and get smooth gameplay! Which is great for a \\"mid-ranged\\" graphics card.

* NICE display! It won't blow you away or anything; however, the 1600 X 900 screen resolution makes games, movies and YouTube look pretty good!

* TREMENDOUS price! Most gaming laptops can cost over $1300 or more! But for around $900 you're getting an OUTSTANDING deal!

And now for the bad:

CONS:

* The lid is a little flimsy. I'm not saying it's going to break on you, but it's not the sturdiest lid I ever seen on a laptop.

* The disc tray is also a little flimsy. Again, it's not going to break on you, I just think it's worth mentioning.

* The battery isn't the best. I can only get about 4 hours off of this laptop when I'm using the battery. And that's not including when I'm playing games. If you ONLY want to get a laptop so you don't have to keep it plugged in for more than 4 hours at a time. Then DON'T get this laptop! Though keep in mind, that with MOST powerful gaming laptops (not including Alien Were) you're going to have a crappy battery life, no matter what.

In conclusion, this is a nearly PERFECT laptop and with such a low price with great system specs. There shouldn't be much holding you back from getting this laptop!you don't have to keep it plugged in for more than 4 hours at a time. Then DON'T get this laptop! Though keep in mind, that with MOST powerful gaming laptops (not including Alien Were) you're going to have a crappy battery life, no matter what.

In conclusion, this is a nearly PERFECT laptop and with such a low price with great system specs. There shouldn't be much holding you back from getting this laptop!

sentence:
Let's set the record straight on this refurbished laptop. I've been a computer power user for almost 15 years. For the price you pay, this thing is a dream. You're saving $200+ over box retail on a very high-performance laptop for its size and weight. I can chew through HD video on the go with this thing and it doesn't wince. No, you're not going to be able to play the latest Steam games with high graphics settings, but for those on the go with student or professional applications this machine is perfect.

In the box are two things: The laptop and the charger. That's it. Why on earth would you want all the promotional papers and throwaways that come with a normal laptop from the electronics store? It's a laptop. It doesn't require a manual or all that trial software that you'll never use anyway. Speaking of which, the amount of trial and promotional software (bloatware) that I had to uninstall was minimal. Thank you, Asus.

I will note that this is the second of these laptops that I've been through. The first one had a bad hard drive (kept locking up and screeching after a few hours of use) so I sent it back and got another. No big deal. Bum hard drives are somewhat common in refurbished laptops and it comes with the territory. It's an easy process thanks to Amazon's great returns policy. Just make sure you use it for a few days to test it out before you throw away the box and make sure you back up your files, as you should anyway.

Let's get straight to the pros and cons.

Pros...
- In mint condition and has no signs of being a refurbished machine
- Lightning-fast startup time for a laptop with a hard drive
- Great performance and specs for the price
- Backlit keyboard is wonderful in the dark
- Keyboard types comfortably for the size and it doesn't bend in the middle unless you apply an unreasonable amount of pressure to it
- 3 USB ports (2 USB 3.0, 1 USB 2.0)
- Fairly quiet fan noise
- Battery life meets the 7 hour advertised length when in a balanced or power saving mode
- 8gb of RAM is a nice surprise in a laptop of this price point
- Charger has an angled plug that goes into the laptop. Easier cord management and prevents accidental bumps.

Cons...
- Charger is small but bulky enough that it may cover up two outlets of a power strip. Outlets should be alright.
- Speakers are lousy. Bring a pair of earbuds or external speakers along if you want decent sound.
- It's a little clunky and heavy for use as a tablet. Buy this thing if you want a laptop that has a touchscreen, not as a tablet replacement.
- One port serves as a headphone jack and microphone input combined. USB microphones can circumvent this.
- Webcam is grainy and not of terribly high resolution.

The pros outweigh the cons easily here. This refurbished laptop is one I recommend!
label: Positive Review

sentence: My 3 year old 10 inch Acer dropped and died. The MacBook is 4 times more expensive, and has only half(128gb) the hard drive space, two criteria that made me ponder the decision extensively, especially because one of my main reasons for getting one was to store photos on my trips. Now that I have been using it a while, I could hardly be more pleased. It is so much smoother a machine than the Acer, has the great start-up speed of ssd, a better monitor, useful back-lit keyboard, superior standard photo software, runs very, very cool in comparison, is totally quiet, is even a tad lighter, and has advanced track pad functions that don't have a mind of their own. I loved my little Acer, but this is a wonderful upgrade indeed.

sentence:
I bought a NEW HD+ directly from Barnes & Noble, and delivered directly to me.

I used it for ~30 days to make sure it was OK, then moved my work files onto it.

I was completing final edit on a production piece about 60 days later. It bricked.
Just instantly black screen, white 'NOOK' logo slowly fading to 'Ha, ha, ..sucker."

Plugged it into the charger. Nothing. Tried '2-buttons for 20 seconds' reboot. Zip.
Plugged it into my PC to see if I could get my files back off. Nothing. BRICKED.

B&N advertises a ONE YEAR WARRANTY with FULL REPLACEMENT. Whew!!

Contacted B&N 800- warranty number. Ended up talking with a 3rd-party service
contractor in South America on a dial-up. [crackle, crackle] 'Si, senor, we need
your credit card number to ship you a new unit.' OK. 'Hold for your RIN number."

Then the line went dead. I called right back. A guy answered this time, "Que?'

'Uh, yeah, I just left my credit card and shipping address but didn't get a RIN.'
'Bueno, diga me your e-mail and we'll send it to you. Puero, what's your DOB?'

I hung up and of course, no RIN came, so I had to call my credit card company
and say my credit information had been compromised by a B&N 3rd-party con.

Wait, it gets better!!

Over the next MONTH I contacted B&N online Customer Service SIX TIMES.
Each time I sent them ALL information on the product, model, serial number,
shipping invoice, order number, credit card, shipping address, ...everything.

EVERY TIME they claimed that their records didn't show me as the purchaser.
I HAD ALL THE RECEIPTS AND EVEN SENT THEM TO THEM, REPEATEDLY.

The Breakthrough!!

Today, B&N contacted me and said 'Your unit is our (sic) of warranty. We will
be happy to ship you a REFURBISHED unit, at substantially REDUCED COST."

I only had the unit 90 days before it bricked. Their warranty is for ONE YEAR!
IN OTHER WORDS, the 'NEW' (sic) UNIT THEY SOLD ME WAS REFURBISHED!!
DON'T PAY FULL VALUE FOR A REFURBISHED UNIT THAT'S GONNA BRICK!!

Don't buy from B&N unless you're just throwing your money into a hole in S.A.

Run Forrest, Run!!
label: Negative Review

I've spent such a long time looking for something that would provide me with day to day memory usage but things always failed. This one is the keeper:

1. sturdy. This is brushed aluminum so it won't fall apart and can sustain itself, unlike all the plastic knobby deals out there. To top it all off, it's one solid piece, which is always ideal.

3. price. More than enough, I'm getting one for all my family members for christmas this year.

4. portability. There is some genius, simple design here where this usb key can easily go anywhere: buy a mini-carabener and then put it anywhere, keychain it knowing it can't be damaged, put it through a lanyard and then attach to phone. . . the possibilities are endless.

The only MAJOR con is that this is the soon-to-be phased out USB 2.0 and I WISH this IDENTICAL model could be USB 3.0. Still this covers my needs 100% for now and am incredibly happy with the purchase.

sentence:
This keyboard has several design flaws which I will outline here.

1. Amount of force needed to press the keys is too high. This causes the user to pound the keys.

2. Keyboard seems to miss double leters effectively. Probably caused by #1 above. >>>Notice that I left a letter out of 'letters' in the sentence before. That was not on purpose!<<< I hit 'l' two times, but only got one. Hapens al the time unless I POUND the double letters intentionally. And again, the missed leters here ('Hapens', 'al') are hapening naturally.

3. The keyboard is very loud and clacky as another reviewer mentioned. Probably caused by #1 above. I sit near a person at work that uses this keyboard and it is LOUD. She said her fingers have gotten stronger since using this keyboard, probably because she has to pound the keys. I'm actually considering buying her another keyboard out of my own pocket... Here at home, as I type this, I'm two rooms away from my sleeping wife, and I'm actually wondering if my typing will wake her up.

4. They keys are recessed in a flush enclosure that has a rigid rim around the base of the keys. If you accidentally hit the edge of the left shift key for example, you'll hit the rigid edge, which is at the same elevation, and miss the shift. This is the first such keyboard I've ever used with this design peculiarity. I now know why keyboard keys should be raised instead of recessed into a flush case.

On a positive note, it looks good. So if you are going for looks only, this is the keyboard for you.

sentence:
Greetings Everyone,

I bought this motherboard to replace an ASUS Rampage IV Extreme I bought on Craigslist dirt cheap. Unfortunately that one would not accept a bios update without first installing an i7-3000 series LGA 2011 processor. I read in some forums that some would flash via USB and some would not regardless of what ASUS said.
My EVGA X79 Classified did not post initially upon install. However, I bought this board for four reasons: Price, EVGA's United States Based Customer Service, The XL-ATX Form Factor, and reports that this board would work with my Intel i7-4930k right out of the box! I registered the motherboard online through EVGA's website and then called EVGA. Within two minutes I was talking to a tech support rep that spoke perfect English. I told him my problem and he asked if my motherboard box had 4th Generation CPU (Ivy Bridge-E) Ready written on it? I responded yes, and he told me to flip a bios switch at the bottom of the board to the left. Viola!!! It booted up flawlessly!!!! I have a Crucial 960GB SSD and it seemed like my Windows 7 Ultimate 64Bit installed in less than ten minutes!!!
This motherboard has an interesting layout. I have a super-sized Cooler Master V8 GTS CPU Cooler and an EVGA GTX 690 video card. The X79 Classified is one of the few mid-entry level boards that accept a V8 GTS cooler without covering the PCI-E x/16 slot. However, my GTX 690 covers both the PCI-E x/1 and front audio header due to the board's layout. I can move the video card to the lower X/16 slot, but that puts it closer to the PSU and affects cooling air flow. So for right now, I'm without front panel sound until I get a sound card or move the video card. No biggie, since I use the system as a hybrid HTPC and gaming PC.
The EVGA GUI Bios is amazing!!! You can literally control every input and output on this board. Everything from fan headers, slot voltages, to SATA ports are controllable through the bios.
The LED post code trouble shooting display is a must for every computer. It should be a federal requirement!
This board skimped on internal USB 3.0 headers (only one) and the SATA ports by giving you only six, four at SATA II and two at SATA III. I bought cards to remedy both of those issues at less than $50 total. The red and black XL board looks great in my Cooler Master Storm Trooper Case with the clear side panel upgrade. A standard ATX motherboard would just be wrong for any full tower case with a clear side panel.
I think EVGA is friendlier to a wider range of RAM manufacturers than ASUS. My previously owned Rampage IV Extreme did not take well to my 16GB of Avexir Yellow LED RAM. I tried the RAM in my Gigabyte motherboard on another one of my HTPCs and I had the same problem. The red LED CPU cooler and yellow RAM combination makes a nice pseudo flame effect inside the case. I’ve gotten tons of compliments from friends and family.
Overall, if you have an Ivy Bridge-E processor, you cannot go wrong with this motherboard. It has less SATA and DIMM slots than EVGA's top of the line model, but adding $50 worth of cards brought me up to 99.5% of that board's capability under Widows 7 64Bit while saving me $80. The XL-form factor was a deal breaker for a full size clear panel case. I had to have function, form, and features at a reasonable price, and the X79 Classified came through with flying colors!
Thanks EVGA!!!

sentence: **Update: The SD slot is failing after 10 months of use. I have reinstalled the operating system three or four times already due to this issue.  Spend the extra money on a real laptop.  This one is garbage.**  Tiny hard drive needs to be augmented with an SD card, which is also available on Amazon.  After doing a system restore, there were 11.7 GB free. Win 8.1 simply does not belong on a 19 GB hard drive (32 GB minus 10 GB fixed recovery drive minus 3GB mystery).  The recovery drive cannot be removed even after copying it to a USB memory stick.  The system restore will ask if you want to delete the recovery partition but will not actually permit you to delete the recovery partition even when restoring from a USB drive. Finally, the mousepad constantly stops working.  Having a wireless mouse on hand is essential.  After 7 months the mousepad buttons failed permanently.  Save your money. sentence:
Wow! This is a powerful router with tons of options, but you do not have to be a network pro to set it up. It just works. Setup is simple and then when it works, it increased range substantially over the Apple Airport that I was running. I hope to eventually set it up to work in tandem with my Airport; I will report here on the results of those efforts.

Overall, there are just a bunch of pro's to report with this wi-fi router
-Incredible range (tri-band allows for super fast short range and longer-range too).
-Not only does it work far away but it works consistently. 6 antennas make for no observed deadzones.
-Fast. Wifi theoretically as fast as wired connection. Most devices honestly don't feel the 1.3 Gbps max speed for internet because internet speeds don't approach the gigabit speeds in most situations (soon Cox will be installing gigabit internet in my region so I am looking forward to maxing speeds out). With the right devices you may feel it with in-network transfers
-USB 3.0 outlet takes advantage of fast wifi speeds to have always connected external hard drive connected via the network. (also has a USB 2.0 plug which I have not used)
-USB allows USB printer to become a wireless printer. This is not a unique feature, but a powerful one if you need it. I do not have any wired printers to have tested this with.
-Backup software uses USB-connected hard drive to automatically backup my pc. I wish that my Mac would do it, but this is a limitation with the Mac not the router. If anybody knows how to automatically backup a Mac to this (like time machine) let me know.

Cons
-Size. The router is very large. I had to be creative on where I placed it.
-Cost. It is expensive, but having consistent and fast internet and network connectivity should be worth the money for most people. It's expensive, but worth it in my estimation.

sentence:
Do not get this product. I am lucky that I got this on sale at the price it was at. I thought with the two fans (woopdiedoo on the lights) and having that extra USB port that it was going to be a good product for the work that I do on my laptop. Sadly, it was not and I am writing this review after having this product for two months.

First lets start out with construction. Great on the top with the mesh. That's it. Otherwise it is horrible! The bottom is plastic and the lifter to be able to have the laptop at in angle was great for the first week. Literally on the 10th day the plastic snapped in half. It isn't like I have that heavy of a laptop in the first place (13" Macbook Pro). For me that's a minor issue. As long as the fans keep on working I am perfectly content. Well within the first month I had this the "lights" went out and the far left fan kicked the bucket. So I am down to one fan and finally that kicks the bucket at the end of February. I tried it on my friends laptop just in case to see if it was something to do with my laptop. Nope.

The other issue I had with it was the usb ports. It seemed to me that they eventually got loose within three weeks of having this product. Since it was loose, it seemed like that if I moved the cooler at all, it wouldn't read what was in the port and at this point I felt like if I breathed on the cooler that the usb port would not work.

Pros:
Two USB ports
Noise level of fans

Cons:
Not the greatest construction
Breaks easily
Died on me completely within two months
Loose USB ports

I took a gamble on this product and lost. I would never recommend this product to anyone in need of a laptop cooler.
label: Negative Review

sentence: I recently purchased a late 2008 - 2009 Macbook Pro w/ 512MB 9600M GT video card. While playing games such as the new Prince of Persia under the High Performance settings the laptop would get extremely hot, and the game would begin to lag.    Here are the temps(while playing PoP) according to iStat widget:  HDD:39 CPU:96 Enclose Base:34 Enc. Base (2):34 Encl. Base(3):33 GPU:75 GPU Diode: 83 GPU Heatsink: 66    Temps After using the Cooler Master Infinite:  HDD:35 CPU:87 Enclose Base:33 Enc. Base (2):32 Encl. Base(3):32 GPU:72 GPU Diode: 78 GPU Heatsink: 58    (Temperatures are in Celcius)    As you can see, the Cooler Master made a HUGE difference, especially in the CPU, and GPU. My game no longer lagged, and I was not worried about frying my $2500 computer.    I did a lot of research before buying this laptop cooler and I am glad I made the jump. The biggest selling point was that it did not draw in air from the bottom of the cooler, because I usually have it on an uneven surface such as my bed or sofa (which would have totally defeated the purpose of the cooler).    The Cooler Master is slightly larger in the front, but fits my Macbook Pro perfectly width wise. Some people complain that it is too loud, but I barely hear it; I don't mind the little noise that I do hear as long as it cools my laptop! The black finish and overall quality of the product is outstanding as well and does not detract from the looks of my laptop at all.    All in all, I would say that I am extremely happy with the purchase, and would highly recommend it to anyone that needs a cooler laptop.
label: Positive Review

sentence:
When I installed the part, it took 2-3 minutes for my PC to boot compared to a normal 20-30 second boot off an SSD. Other reviews had stated incompatibility with a UEFI BIOS interface which I also have. Once the part was installed and I booted up, there was a series of numbers and letters that would flash in the lower right corner before the OS booted (Win 7) which I have never seen before. Once I was in the OS, the part was never detected. I went to the website and no drivers could be found.

Just in case, I tried the other connector on the motherboard to see if that would make a difference. This time when I booted I smelled something burning and immediately turned off the PC to take the part out. I was unable to start the PC up again after. I took the PC to a local PC repair chain to have a diagnostic done and it ended up frying my motherboard. At this time I don't know if the processor is shot.

I contacted Rosewill at first to see if a resolution could be met. I explained in the first email what I have explained in this review thus far. After I explained again what happened, Rosewill contacted Newegg (I stated I bought the part on Amazon in the first email) and I had to reiterate the issue again. After I provided the order number again they offered to send a replacement part and I had stated that I can no longer use the part (nor would I want to) as the part ruined my motherboard. They again apologized and said that they will send the part, that I don't have to send the damaged one back, and that I would have to contact Amazon regarding the refund. Not once did Rosewill acknowledge or address that their hardware caused the damage it did. I still await their next response but I'm already out at least $100-150 for the diagnostic, the replacement, and the installation of the new motherboard. Possibly more if the processor is ruined too.
label: Negative Review

sentence:
I was sent this mouse by Anker to test in return for my honest review.

I ordered this mouse to test not for the gaming qualities it offers, but because I wanted a quality product, backed up by a wonderfully helpful customer service and the 18 month guarantee.

I have tried a number of units in the past and all of them have driven me mad. In a very short time the old roller system stops working and you then had to drag the mouse many times across the pad to make it work or the optical system stopped working and constantly needed resetting. With this mouse, I have had no issues whatsoever. It is smooth and has worked without a hitch. It is quite a large unit, but it fits my hand comfortably. It has a really cool Anker Logo light that can be changed to a multitude of colours. It comes with a software package which is better than anything Microsoft has created for its products. It is extremely precise and the DPI can be easily changed depending upon what you want. You can also use two separate profiles for different users, which is changed on the base of the mouse. It comes with very clear instructions and is easy to use. I am still finding the different settings this mouse has, but so far I have found very few drawbacks. The only problem I have found with this product is that it is a wired mouse, although it does come with a very long cable, other than this issue I love the product.

At the given price, I think you will be hard bit to find a better mouse that has all these features.

Highly Recommended.
label: Positive Review

sentence:
Be very careful about buying an HP desktop. I'm currently attempting, through both the Better Business Bureau and my state attorney general's office, to obtain a refund for a defective top of the line HP desktop purchased new in November 2009. Since then, have spent a hundred hours or more trying to work through black screen freezes, sleep issue freezes, sleep runaway graphic card fan issues, and finally, a complete failure of the computer to recognize the RAID configuration, & resulting inability to access hard drives. With the latest failure, I spent five hours on a vacation day with their often unintelligible tech support, who finally wanted me to attempt to overwrite my hard drives - which were perfectly good - thus destroying all my data. 1) this wouldn't work, as the system couldn't access them, 2) I wasn't going to destroy my data. I was then referred to a supervisor, who tried to walk me through the same steps. I again refused to attempt an overwrite of my hard drives. Was then referred to a case manager, who, although I did not ask to escalate, kept repeating \\"I am the case manager, you cannot escalate beyond me.\\" She wanted me to send in the computer, and I wanted a refund. We hung up. Over the next week, I removed my hard drives and copied the data over, then wiped the drives. I called HP back, spent an hour waiting for referred 2nd level, who said I should have called the case manager, that he couldn't help. Told him I didn't have contact info; he said I should have been given it, but he'd contact her and I'd hear back in a few days. That was March 11. Today is April 23. Still waiting.

Filed a complaint with the Better Business Bureau. HP finally contacted me nearly three weeks after they received the complaint from the Better Business Bureau. They called while I was at work, then sent a follow-up e-mail. I arrived home 1/2 hour after their call and immediately returned their call. Was told I couldn't speak to the case manager who called me because he was getting off work in five minutes, so they'd assign a 2nd shift case manager who would call me back. I hung up, then responded to the e-mail advising that I'd returned the phone call and was waiting for a return call. That was April 15. Today is April 23. Still waiting.

Two days ago, HP followed up with the BBB, advising - get this - that they'd called me and sent me an e-mail and I hadn't responded, but that I should call them if I \\"still have an issue.\\" So, I responded through the BBB, reminding them that I had in fact returned their phone call, was advised I'd be called back, and I responded to their e-mail. And, you guessed it, I'm still waiting.

In the meantime, I've also filed a complaint with my state's attorney general's office. If neither the BBB or attorney general's office can get HP to respond, then my next step is small claims court.

The moral of the story: If you purchase an HP computer, hope nothing goes wrong.was getting off work in five minutes, so they'd assign a 2nd shift case manager who would call me back. I hung up, then responded to the e-mail advising that I'd returned the phone call and was waiting for a return call. That was April 15. Today is April 23. Still waiting. Two days ago, HP followed up with the BBB, advising - get this - that they'd called me and sent me an e-mail and I hadn't responded, but that I should call them if I \\"still have an issue.\\" So, I responded through the BBB, reminding them that I had in fact returned their phone call, was advised I'd be called back, and I responded to their e-mail. And, you guessed it, I'm still waiting. In the meantime, I've also filed a complaint with my state's attorney general's office. If neither the BBB or attorney general's office can get HP to respond, then my next step is small claims court. The moral of the story: If you purchase an HP computer, hope nothing goes wrong.

sentence:
I used to always bottom out my Razer BlackWidow with MX Blues. I like the click and clack noises but I have a friend with the o rings and I really liked them, so I decided to buy them. It took a really long time to do all the keys, but man is it worth it. I'm getting used to the lack of a clack noise, but because they're MX blues, the keys themselves are still very tactile and clicky, I'm not really missing out on anything. It doesn't make the keys mushy or bad in any way. I'm really glad I got the red o rings because I swear the distance difference is actually pretty noticeable, I wouldn't get the blue o-rings unless you want super short key travel.

Note if you have a Razer BlackWidow (mine's a pre 2013 model), the +, enter, and 0 key on the numberpad have stabilizer bars that are facing the wrong way so they're damn near impossible to remove or put back. They unclip on the edge of the keyboard instead of toward the inside of the keyboard. I didn't bother with those keys since I really don't care about the number pad, although it's kind of irritating knowing that I couldn't get every single key. If you're super OCD just a forewarning.

Also, it takes a while to put these suckers on. Make sure you have a good key puller, I recommend WASD's key puller, although this is entirely up to your preference. Also, I wouldn't bother trying to put the o rings on with your fingers, even if you're really nimble. I took a pen and unscrewed the top and it fit perfectly to push on all the o rings. It took a while but if I tried to do it any other way I imagine it would've taken forever.

Even though the price is a little steep, as everyone else is saying, it's worth it. If you're even here looking at o rings for your mechanical keyboard, you know what you're doing and you know exactly what you want. Go for it.

EDIT after a few months of use:

So, today I was cleaning my mechanical keyboard and decided to take off the O-rings. I just bought another keyboard with ALPS switches that are very, very clicky and I wanted to go back to the original feel of regular MX blue switches while the ALPS keyboard is shipping. That isn't to say I entirely disliked the O-rings. I can see how they're awesome if you have a linear switch and you want to shorten your key travel distance. For that--they're perfect; and if you want a switch to be less noisy if you're in an office environment they would be great as well. However, over time, I missed the clack of the MX Blues. It's weirdly soothing.

I'm not saying anything bad about the product, as they did their job perfectly. I tried them and simply discovered I did miss the bottoming out noise, so I went back to normal. If you know for a fact you like your keyboards to be noisy, you will probably enjoy the difference this product makes for a while, just for the sake of change, then you might go back to wonderfully noisy switches. Just know what you're buying and really consider whether or not this would be right for you.st bought another keyboard with ALPS switches that are very, very clicky and I wanted to go back to the original feel of regular MX blue switches while the ALPS keyboard is shipping. That isn't to say I entirely disliked the O-rings. I can see how they're awesome if you have a linear switch and you want to shorten your key travel distance. For that--they're perfect; and if you want a switch to be less noisy if you're in an office environment they would be great as well. However, over time, I missed the clack of the MX Blues. It's weirdly soothing.

I'm not saying anything bad about the product, as they did their job perfectly. I tried them and simply discovered I did miss the bottoming out noise, so I went back to normal. Just know what you're buying and really consider whether or not this would be right for you.

After that, the system will not post unless I shut off the power at the PSU for more than 10 seconds.

We are currently running a memory test (Memtest86) to check the ram, as that would be my first guess based on the nature of the problems but so far no errors are showing. I am inclined to think there is something wrong with the mobo at this point.

sentence: Ordered this netbook because I was looking for an n450 powered device akin to the 1005 line.     Well let me say that I'm glad I didn't spend the extra 60 bucks. This computer has everything the 1005 line has minus bluetooth (honestly, I don't think I've ever met a person who uses bluetooth) battery life (11 vs 14 hours, real world 8 vs 11 hours), harddrive space (160GB versus 250GB which is a negligible difference), display (matte screen vs glossy screen equal resolution), wireless (g vs n) and finish (matte vs glossy).     Now, that seems like a lot of differences, but all of them, save battery life, are pointless bells and whistles that do nothing to affect the performance of the computer (akin to putting a moon roof and a spoiler on a car and jacking the price up).     Battery life is the only worthwhile difference between the two models (maybe worth 60-80 bucks depending on what your plans are). It would have been nice to have wireless n, but most users probably won't notice a difference.    As far as the screen and finish go, I think matte is actually an advantage. A glossy screen is exhausting to read on in bright light. I'll never understand what the recent obsession with glossy screens is; they are murderous to the eyes. As for a glossy finish, your netbook will look like a piece of well worn packing tape after 15 minutes (big greasy fingerprints everywhere!).     I've heard a lot of people complain that the keyboard of this model feels spongy, but I think they must be typing with mallets, because the 1001p keyboard feels like every other laptop I've ever used. It does not depress in the corners or anywhere for that matter and I'm a fairly hard typist.     The first thing I did when I got this computer was clean install windows 7 ultimate and office 2003 (office 2007 being an unholy abomination; a grievous injustice perpetrated upon all of mankind). So far the netbook has been breezing through applications on 1GB ram with nary a stutter. I would highly recommend upgrading from the absolute garbage windows 7 starter, it doesn't even let you change the wallpaper and is solely intended to annoy you. Furthermore, you might as well get rid of all the asus software (it periodically generates pop-up ads asking if you want to pay for something) and just install the drivers for lan and wireless, you really don't need the others.     In conclusion (ah, high school english where would I be without you) The 1001P is a solid, surprisingly powerful little netbook with excellent design. It is more than adequate for most computing tasks short of video editing. And for 280$? Are you kidding me? How can you complain?

sentence:
For those of us who grew up with FM receivers the size of a PC, this is a revelation. The radio looks more like an antenna than an FM Stereo tuner. A good deal of the compact size comes from the fact that all of the amplification is located in your PC or your powered speakers. The cables need to be hooked up to a USB port, as well as either a Line-In or Mic-In connector on your sound card. I can't find fault with the hardware, but the software has been a nightmare.
I've installed the radio on two Windows 98 machines and a Windows 2000 Professional machine with miserable results. I was never able to get on-screen volume controls to work under Windows 98, and after several weeks all of the software quit working entirely. Under windows 2000 Pro, everything worked O.K. for a couple of months, then I started getting an error message on start up that said that a number must be between 1 and (about 2 billion). All of the station names disappeared, and the volume controls in the D-Link software stopped working. Upgrading to new drivers resulted in the software being unable to locate the radio, even though Windows said that it was there and working correctly. Uninstalling the software still resulted in Windows trying to start it on boot up. Reinstalling the software was unsuccessful, as I kept receiving the 'unable to locate radio' message.
So, now I'm left with a [dollar amount] paperweight. Perhaps this is the reason that you don't see a lot of USB radios on the market. While it is a great concept, the software is definitely \\"not ready for prime time\\". If you really need to integrate a radio into your PC, you should probably look elsewhere.

sentence: I purchased this to drive 3 Toshiba IK-WB15A network cameras.  The cameras are supposed to be 802.3af compliant and are successfully driven by individual Linksys POE injectors, but could not be driven by this Netgear  FS108PNA POE switch.  This switch is supposed to be 802.3af compliant as well, providing up to 15.4 watts of power per POE port, except that it is supposed to have a maximum POE capability of 32 watts total.  According to Toshiba the cameras pull approximately 10 watts.  All of this adds up to this switch should drive 2 of these network cameras, but not 3.  In reality, this switch will on drive one camera.  When a second camera is attached it appears to drive it for a few moments, but then the second camera drops offline and will never power back up, even though the switch indicates that it is providing power on the second camera port, and the amber light indicating that the POE power available is below 5 watts never flashed.  If a third camera is connected, it will never power on, and the amber light flashes as expected.  I am an engineer and I spoke to both the Toshiba and linksys tech support engineers who claim that their products are 802.3af compliant and that the switch should drive two of the cameras.  They just claim that the fault lies with the other manufacturers equipment.  I tested all 3 cameras, and even purchased a second Linksys FS108PNA switch and obtained the same results.  Between all of this testing and the fact that my cameras are successfully driven by individual linksys POE injectors, I can only conclude that the cameras actually draw more than 10 watts or that the Linksys POE switch simply does not provide enough POE power capability.  Bottom line is do not purchase this product to drive a network camera unless you have some way to test your camera with this switch, or you can find someone who has already done so, or you only plan on driving one network camera and nothing else on this POE switch.  I was not able to return my switches, as ANTONLINE the seller would not refund my money.

NO WARRANTY - DO NOT BUY!

I bought one of these modems from NewEgg just for internet.b Time Warner sent out a couple of free lance installers. They opened the modem from the box and attempted installation. I got NO INTERNET. The installers claimed the fault was the modem. I called Motorola support. Motorola had me bring up their IP addressed program. I was told that some of the data indicated that Time Warner was technically within specs, but near the limit of being out of spec. Motorola blamed Time Warner, saying that the IP report indicated that the modem was probably all right.

Today I got a shocker! I read A manual on the Modem and particularly looked at the "Warranty," which is a NON-WARRANTY!!!

Quote: "A limited warranty for this Product (including Software) is provided by Motorola to your
distributor, cable operator, or Internet service provider, as applicable. Please contact your cable operator or Internet
service provider (“Service Provider”) for details. Motorola does not warrant that any Software will perform error-free or
without bugs. . . .
MOTOROLA DOES NOT WARRANT THIS PRODUCT DIRECTLY TO YOU, THE END USER."

I cannot believe it -- Motorola gives an expressed NON-WARRANTY to the user.

But then I read a piece of paper in the modem box which seems to say there is a 1 year warranty on the hardware, but at best replacement. I phoned them & was told that they do not give money back.

The first day with this modem & TW, I got no internet.
The 2nd day I got internet at snail speed 0.1 Mbps (Ookla).
The 2nd day I replaced it with a Zoom modem & got very fast internet!
Unfortunately, the Motorola unit is not heavy enough to use as a boat anchor!

sentence:
This is a great high quality keyboard. The switches feel very light and fluid with a slight tactile feedback, but I can also rest my hands on the keys themselves without depressing them. The key tops are shaped differently from probably any other keyboard, and the edges take some getting used to. As other users have said, the key tops make the key spacing feel slightly different, but the spacing is actually about the same (if not exactly the same) as is standard for Logitech. The key laser etching looks fantastic, and the lighting for each key is confined only to that key with a very uniform brightness.

The keyboard feels nice and weighty - not necessarily heavy, but it feels like it is durable. The quality of plastic used feels very high quality, not like the standard plastic you find in most keyboards. As stated, it comes with two palm rests. The one typically pictured is good for gaming, but it forces your hands into a slightly asymmetric position, unsuitable for typing. The second, smaller palm rest is the one I keep on the keyboard. It is wide enough for a guy with fairly large hands. It is also what you would want to use for typing, as it keeps your hands positioned more symmetrically.

Overall, I am highly pleased with my purchase and would recommend this keyboard to anyone looking for a high end mechanical keyboard. It may be pricey, but it is very worth it.

sentence:
As I'm writing this, I'm desperately trying to salvage files from a defective unit. After using this unit with Raid 0 configuration for 5 months with GUID partition table and HFS+ with journaling, I started to run into HFS+ journal corruption. Considering this a fluke and perhaps the fault of the OS, I corrected the issue using Apple's hfsutils to restart the partition's journal. Within a month of this incident the drive became ripe with IOerrors at the physical layer. Some days the drive would be stuck on the G-Technology Red Blink of Death with a light and repetitive \\"click\\" sound, and this could only be resolved by holding the drive to a specific angle until the red blink and click went away and the drives re-initialized.

These units, while fast and effective at first, tire quickly. In the G-RAID Mini series, the drives do not power down when you shut your computer down (even with a full ACPI halt). If using the USB option, the drives will only spin down once the AC/DC transformer is disconnected. When connected via a powered IEEE 1394b connection, as long as your PC or Macintosh's power supply is powering peripherals, the drives are still working. When your data is being trusted to Hitachi \\"Deathstar\\" drives that can be on for days-on-end, it's no wonder this series encounters problems.

Do not rely on this drive and definitely do not rely on G-Technology's warranty; it covers a storage device and not your data. G-Technology does not repair drives nor does it attempt any data restoration service in the case of failure. If you get the Red Blink of Death, they will simply send you a new drive if you're lucky enough to be within warranty.
label: Negative Review

sentence: I am no network specialist but I can honestly say that I did try everything to make this device work - everything within reason! There are EXTRA SPECIAL youtube videos and pages dedicated to the problems of configuring various TPLink routers like this one. I tried running the totally useless CD (which says everything is working but isn't AT ALL), standard configs, exotic configs, firmware updates and nothing worked. My Dlink 655 (which I configured myself as well as numerous other wired and wireless routers) has been rock solid but I would still like dual band for my growing home network and the ability to run my own FTP with multiple surveillance cameras. I has high hopes for this unit and felt that I took a positive chance on an unfamiliar brand. I guess It's gonna be "name brands" from here on out - I'll give the Netgear WNDR4300 a shot. Now here's the good,,, the unit is visually attractive, which is all the more disappointing because it "really" matched my setup. All it had to do was connect to the internet to make me happy - it didn't!
label: Negative Review

sentence: Worked perfect on two different Dell computers, one Optiplex and one Vostro, both with Windows 7.  Easy to install the pci express card.  The driver cd is somewhat unstable.  I had problems with both computers in which I had to re-run the cd twice on each.  You might be better off downloading a newer driver off the internet if available.  Overall it works great as the customer wanted dual monitors with extended desktops.  The resolution is super good as well as the on card memory.  It does come with two additional mounts in the event you want to install it into a low profile computer case.  I am happy with it, customer is thrilled with it, I do highly recommend it.  Excellent price.
label: Positive Review

sentence: I have been a Western Digital customer for year buying different models and currently use the 1TB My Book Office edition.  I have heard bad things about the 1TB My book HOME edition but ignored the warnings before I bought one.  Last week, my HOME edition external drive would not come on.  It receives power and the Western Digital customer service says something on my circuit board probably shorted out and I will not be able to retrieve my files.  Isn't the reason you buy a external hard drive is to protect back up and protect your files on your computer.  This model totally defeats its purpose. I'm spending a few extra dollars and buying another model or brand.
